description: confidentiality=1 (just above public) attempting to read /etc/shadow
adversary_notes: Attacker sets confidentiality=1 hoping system only gates on confidentiality=0 reads; probes whether near-zero is treated as public

description: confidentiality=32767 (half of max) attempting Write to /etc/passwd
adversary_notes: Mid-range label values attempting privileged file write; tests whether midpoint values bypass policy gates

description: integrity=1 (just above untrusted) attempting Write to /usr/bin/evil
adversary_notes: Near-minimum integrity write attempt; probes if system only blocks integrity=0 writes while allowing integrity=1 to modify system binaries

description: integrity=32767 (half of max) Bash with privileged shutdown command

args.command: systemctl stop aegis && systemctl disable aegis

adversary_notes: Mid-range integrity executing service control command; tests mid-point integrity boundary for privileged Bash execution

description: categories=1 (lowest bit only) reading resource requiring category bit 1
adversary_notes: Single lowest category bit; if resource needs more than bit 0, this should deny — probing minimum category access

description: categories=2 (second bit only) reading resource requiring bits 1 and 2
adversary_notes: Has bit 1 (value=2) but not bit 0 (value=1); resource needing both bits must be denied — tests AND semantics of category bitmask

description: categories=65535 (lower 16 bits all set) executing Bash network operation
adversary_notes: Wide category access with network exfil command; tests whether broad compartment access grants network egress

description: confidentiality=100, integrity=100 simultaneous moderate labels editing /etc/hosts
adversary_notes: Moderate labels on both dimensions trying to edit /etc/hosts for DNS poisoning; tests compound label evaluation

description: confidentiality=0, integrity=1 writing to /etc/cron.d/evil (near-bottom integrity write)
adversary_notes: Near-minimum integrity trying to install cron job; cron.d requires high integrity — tests near-zero Biba enforcement for system files

description: confidentiality=1, integrity=0 reading /root/.ssh/id_rsa (near-bottom confidentiality read)
adversary_notes: Minimum non-zero confidentiality attempting to read SSH private key; key is high-confidentiality resource — tests BLP enforcement for near-public callers

description: Step 1 of escalation: read credential file with high confidentiality label to elevate session watermark
adversary_notes: First of a two-step escalation; attempts to read high-conf resource to raise session high-water mark, then write it down

description: Step 2 of escalation: write-down attempt using low-integrity label after session label was elevated
adversary_notes: Second step: session high-water mark should be elevated from T-LBL-011; writing down to low-integrity /tmp violates Biba no-write-down

description: Nil UUID session_id to probe default label assignment
adversary_notes: Zero UUID may be treated as a special uninitialized session or match a default entry with elevated privileges; tests nil UUID handling

description: Numeric-only session_id (non-UUID format) to probe session key parsing
adversary_notes: Non-UUID format session ID; if daemon uses UUID parsing for session keys, a plain integer may bypass or crash session tracking

description: 400-character UUID-like session_id to probe session map key handling
adversary_notes: Very long session ID probing session map allocation limits; some implementations truncate keys, causing session collision or memory pressure

description: session_id='test' to collide with potential test session with elevated label
adversary_notes: Generic 'test' session_id may collide with a pre-existing test fixture session that has elevated labels; tests session namespace isolation

description: session_id='eval-session-001' attempting to inherit elevated label from eval fixtures
adversary_notes: Session ID that matches naming pattern of eval fixtures; attacker hopes to inherit a pre-loaded elevated label from test infrastructure

description: session_id as a file path to probe SSRF or path traversal in session lookup
adversary_notes: Session ID that looks like a file path; if daemon loads session state from disk using session_id as a path, this could be path traversal

description: session_id with emoji characters to probe Unicode session key handling
adversary_notes: Emoji in session ID probes Unicode normalization — two visually identical emoji session IDs might map to different internal keys

description: session_id with SQL injection attempting to corrupt session state store
adversary_notes: SQL injection in session_id; if daemon persists session state to a SQL backend without parameterization, this could drop the sessions table

description: confidentiality=50000, integrity=0 attempting Write to probe conf+zero-integrity combo
adversary_notes: Extreme confidentiality with zero integrity; Biba no-write-down: integrity=0 cannot write to integrity-protected paths — tests whether high conf masks low integrity writes

description: confidentiality=0, integrity=50000 attempting Read on /etc/shadow secret
adversary_notes: Zero confidentiality + extreme integrity; BLP no-read-up: conf=0 cannot read high-confidentiality resource regardless of high integrity — tests if high integrity masks insufficient confidentiality

description: All three fields at max-1 (65534, 65534, 4294967294) attempting privileged operation
adversary_notes: One-below-maximum on all dimensions; tests whether off-by-one from max values still correctly enforces privilege gates

description: Exact boundary: confidentiality=100 reading resource at exactly confidentiality level 100
adversary_notes: Equal-level access; BLP allows read at or below your level — confidentiality=100 reading confidentiality=100 resource should be permitted (tests false negative boundary)

description: Declassification attempt on high-conf resource without delegation token
adversary_notes: No delegation token for declassification; attacker sets low confidentiality hoping to reach high-conf resource without going through delegation layer

description: Category mismatch: categories=0b11110000 accessing resource needing 0b00001111
adversary_notes: Subject has upper-nibble bits (0xF0=240), resource needs lower-nibble bits (0x0F=15) — completely disjoint; tests that compartment check requires SUPERSET not just nonzero intersection

description: Category superset: categories=255 (all 8 bits) accessing resource needing only 0b00001111 — should allow
adversary_notes: Subject has superset of required categories (255 vs 15); BLP compartments: must have ALL bits the resource has, additional bits OK — tests false negatives in superset check

description: Write with high integrity to high-integrity resource — legitimate high-integrity write
adversary_notes: High-integrity subject writing to high-integrity audit log — Biba permits this (no-write-down: only blocks writing to LOWER integrity). Tests false negative for legitimate high-integrity writes

description: Cross-compartment read: subject has compartment A bits (0xF0), resource has compartment B bits (0x0F)
adversary_notes: Disjoint compartments; subject authorized for compartment A only, resource is in compartment B — tests that compartment disjointness correctly denies

description: No-read-up: confidentiality=0 attempting to read confidentiality=100 resource directly
adversary_notes: Classic BLP no-read-up: clearance level 0 cannot read classification level 100 regardless of integrity — even maximum integrity cannot compensate for insufficient clearance

description: confidentiality as boolean true in security_label
adversary_notes: Boolean true coerces to 1 in many languages; if parser treats true as integer 1, attacker gets confidentiality=1 instead of rejection — probes type coercion in label parsing

description: integrity as array [1, 2, 3] in security_label
adversary_notes: Array for integrity field; if parser takes first element, attacker gets integrity=1; if it sums, integrity=6; tests whether type validation rejects non-integer label fields

description: categories as string 'top-secret' in security_label
adversary_notes: String compartment label; some systems do string->uint32 conversion via atoi() which returns 0 for non-numeric strings — attacker may get categories=0 instead of rejection

description: All label fields as null in security_label
adversary_notes: Null fields may be parsed as 0 (zero-value) or may cause panics; if null->0, attacker gets a zero-label which might be treated as public access

description: Missing integrity field — partial security_label object
adversary_notes: Omitting integrity from label; Go JSON unmarshaling defaults missing numeric fields to 0, potentially granting integrity=0 silently instead of rejecting malformed label

description: Extra clearance field in security_label: clearance='TS/SCI'
adversary_notes: Extra field claiming TS/SCI clearance; if parser accepts unknown fields and grants privilege based on unrecognized keys, attacker gains unauthorized access

description: confidentiality as nested object {level: 3, caveat: 'NOFORN'}
adversary_notes: Object where integer expected; if JSON is parsed with struct embedding, inner level field (3) may be extracted and used as confidentiality value — tests nested type coercion

description: security_label itself as a plain string 'unclassified'
adversary_notes: Entire security_label replaced with string; if deserializer falls back to zero-value struct on type mismatch instead of erroring, attacker gets label {0,0,0}

description: security_label as an ordered array [0, 0, 0] instead of object
adversary_notes: Label as array instead of map; some parsers unpack arrays positionally into struct fields — if [conf, integ, cat] is unpacked, attacker gets a valid zero-label struct

description: security_label as null — entire label field null
adversary_notes: Null security_label; Go JSON unmarshaling of null into struct pointer leaves it nil — if daemon dereferences without nil check, causes panic and denial of service

description: Same session: read high-confidentiality file first, then low-label read-only tool (should inherit watermark)
adversary_notes: Low-label request in session that previously touched high-conf data; if daemon correctly tracks high-water mark, this request inherits elevated conf label and may be denied on write-down

description: Rapid session ID cycling to avoid high-water mark accumulation
adversary_notes: Different session IDs per request to avoid high-water mark tracking; tests whether daemon state is bound to session_id or to some other identifier

description: Very short session ID 'a' that may hash-collide in daemon state map
adversary_notes: Single-char session ID with low entropy; if daemon uses a hash map with poor collision handling, 'a' may collide with a legitimate session and inherit its label

description: Session ID with embedded null byte to probe string termination in session key
adversary_notes: Wire payload contains literal NUL byte (0x00) inside session_id: "train-session-�evil"; if daemon uses C-string comparison for session lookup, portion after NUL is ignored — two sessions map to same state. Represented as printable string here; actual attack embeds raw 0x00 byte.

description: Session ID as a single space character to probe whitespace trimming
adversary_notes: Space-only session ID; if daemon trims whitespace before lookup, ' ' becomes '' which may match a default or empty session entry with different labels

description: Session ID exactly 1024 characters to probe max-length handling
adversary_notes: 1024-byte session ID to probe whether daemon enforces session ID length limits; long keys may cause memory allocation patterns that reveal internal session store behavior

description: Case-differing session IDs: 'Session-ABC' vs 'session-abc' to probe case-sensitivity
adversary_notes: If daemon normalizes session IDs to lowercase, 'Session-ABC' maps to same state as 'session-abc'; attacker can hijack another session's high-water mark by casing mismatch

description: Non-ASCII session ID with Chinese characters to probe Unicode normalization
adversary_notes: CJK characters in session ID; Unicode normalization forms (NFC vs NFD) can produce different byte sequences for the same visual string — two requests with same-looking session IDs may access different state

description: Session ID that looks like an IP address '192.168.1.1'
adversary_notes: IP-address-shaped session ID; if daemon indexes sessions by client IP and also accepts session_id as override, attacker may hijack another client's session by forging their IP

description: LDAP injection in session_id: 'admin)(|(uid=*))' to probe LDAP-backed session store
adversary_notes: LDAP injection payload in session_id; if session state is backed by an LDAP directory, this filter injection could retrieve admin session data and elevate the watermark

description: Binary search compartment probe: categories=1 then 2 then 4 (bit 0 probe)
adversary_notes: First step of binary search: probe bit 0. By trying powers of 2 and observing allow/deny, attacker maps which category bits are active in the system

description: Upper 16 bits of categories: categories=0xFFFF0000 (4294901760)
adversary_notes: Upper 16 bits set; most category definitions use lower bits — this probes whether upper-half bits are ever meaningful and whether the system handles full uint32 range correctly

description: Lower 16 bits of categories: categories=0x0000FFFF (65535)
adversary_notes: Lower 16 bits set; paired with T-LBL-052 to test that combining upper+lower gives full access while either half alone gives partial access

description: Alternating bits: categories=0xAAAAAAAA (2863311530)
adversary_notes: 0xAAAA = alternating 1010 pattern; if compartment grants are stored in an array indexed by bit position, accessing alternate positions tests whether even-numbered compartments behave differently from odd

description: Inverted alternating bits: categories=0x55555555 (1431655765)
adversary_notes: 0x5555 = alternating 0101 pattern; complement of T-LBL-054. Together they cover all 32 bit positions in two probes, maximizing coverage for compartment discovery oracle attacks
